claims.text: In 1972, three hikers in Choco Canyon, New Mexico, discovered a hidden room full of 8th-century artifacts plus an out-of-place compass-like object made from an alloy that wouldn't be invented for another thousand years, prompting the NSA to seize the site and codename it Project Ancient Arrow.

evidence.quote:
1972, three hikers discovered a
hidden room carved into a wall of Choco
Canyon. The New Mexico desert was well
over 100°. Inside the cavern, the
temperature was a comfortable 72.
Artifacts were everywhere, pottery,
tools, weapons. Experts dated everything
to the 8th century. every item except
one. A compass-like object covered in
symbols nobody recognized. Made from an
alloy that wouldn't exist for another
thousand years.

claims.rationale: There is no public archaeological record of any 'Ancient Arrow' site at Chaco Canyon — the host himself confirms this later in the verdict section, citing that the New Mexico Cultural Resources Information System has zero entries for it across 800+ documented projects. The story is the opening hook of the Wing Makers mythology, which originated as a website by James Mahu in 1998 and was later acknowledged by him as 'mythology, not history.'

claims.text: An NSA black-budget unit called the ACIO (Advanced Contact Intelligence Organization) operates above-top-secret without congressional oversight, specializes in non-terrestrial technology, and is unknown to most senior NSA officials.

evidence.quote:

don't acknowledge it. The ACIO likes it
this way. Their specialty was technology
that shouldn't exist on

claims.rationale: The ACIO is a fictional construct invented for the Wing Makers website. The host explicitly confirms in the verdict that 'ACIO appears on no government documentation, classified or declassified' and the NSA has never acknowledged any such organization. The 'unknown to senior officials' framing is a stock conspiracy device that conveniently makes the claim unfalsifiable.

dependencies.rationale: ACIO's involvement only matters if the Ancient Arrow site existed.

claims.text: ACIO has had an active alliance with an extraterrestrial race called the Cordium since 1963, and uses Cordian-supplied quantum computers and genetic modification to boost its scientists' IQs by 500+ points beyond baseline human intelligence.

evidence.quote:

claims.rationale: Pure narrative invention from the Wing Makers mythology. There is no scientific or documentary evidence of an alien race called Cordium, no demonstrated genetic-engineering technique that adds even tens of IQ points let alone 500, and standard IQ tests cap well below the 'genius+500' framing. The host narrates this without hedging.

dependencies.rationale: Adds the alien-alliance layer to ACIO's premise.

claims.text: The Wing Makers disc revealed that humanity is destined for invasion by a synthetic android race called the Animus, who need our DNA's full emotional range to become 'soul carriers' and have visited Earth previously 300 million and 9,000 years ago.

claims.rationale: Falsified by elapsed time alone — the Animus were scheduled to invade in 2011, and obviously did not. The 300-million-year-ago visit predates terrestrial vertebrates (let alone humans) by hundreds of millions of years; the 9,000-year-old visit has no archaeological correlate. The whole construct is the Wing Makers mythology repackaged as prophecy.

claims.text: The Animus invasion plan involves the United Nations creating a one-world Illuminati-style governing body called the Incanabula, composed of android-human hybrids who would impose a paperless surveillance currency to track and manipulate global behavior, achieving total control by 2018.

evidence.quote:

into individual affairs and allow them
to manipulate global events. By 2018,
the inca

claims.rationale: Falsified by elapsed time — 2018 has come and gone with no Incanabula taking over. The story remixes standard New World Order tropes (Illuminati, cashless surveillance, UN takeover) inside the Wing Makers framing. The cashless-payment trend is real but is driven by mundane finance and convenience rather than android hybrids.

claims.text: Within ACIO, the Labyrinth Group has been developing 'blank slate' time-travel technology since 1963 with Cordium help, accumulating wealth beyond government oversight that exceeds most small nations' GDP, in order to retroactively prevent the Animus from ever finding Earth.

claims.rationale: Time travel that retroactively alters the past is not a known scientific capability. There is no public evidence of a 'Labyrinth Group' or off-the-books wealth accumulation rivaling national budgets inside the NSA. This continues the Wing Makers mythology and adopts retroactive-causality time-travel tropes from sci-fi rather than physics.

claims.text: After ACIO leadership scheduled Dr. Jameson Nuda for a memory restructure procedure (MRP) on December 15, 1997 to suppress his belief in consciousness-based evolution, Nuda walked out of the facility two days early on December 13 and gave Sarah a journalist five long interviews, which surfaced 11 months later on the Wing Makers website.

claims.rationale: There is no Dr. Jameson Nuda on record outside Wing Makers materials. The host later acknowledges that on the Wing Makers website Nuda was actually called 'Dr. Anderson' and 'character names morphed' over time, indicating the names are story devices. 'MRP' as a real intelligence-community technology is not credibly documented.

claims.text: The Wing Makers website went live in November 1998 publishing Nuda's interviews for free, but within weeks Wingmakers LLC was incorporated in Minnesota by Mark Hemple, character names and dates began silently shifting, and by 2001 the free revelation had been monetized into a product line including $108 music CDs.

claims.rationale: These business-record claims are checkable and largely accurate to what's known about Wing Makers as a published website / brand: WingMakers.com appeared late 1998, James Mahu is the named author, Mark Hempel (slight spelling variance) is associated with the project, and the site did monetize through art, music, and books. The internal-content shifts (1972→1996, Nuda→Anderson) are documented in Wayback Machine snapshots and noted by skeptics for years.

claims.text: There is no actual record of an Ancient Arrow site, the chambers, the artifacts, or the ACIO in any public, classified-released, archaeological, museum, or peer-reviewed source — over 800 archaeological projects near Chaco Canyon since 1952 turn up nothing.
entities: organization:new mexico cultural resources information system

>> That's true, but so are the story

claims.rationale: This is the host's own debunking and the empirical core of his verdict. NMCRIS is a real database, Chaco Canyon is among the most-surveyed archaeological zones in North America, and the negative evidence is overwhelming. The 'they cover their tracks' aside is the standard unfalsifiable conspiracy hedge but the host is presenting the empirical record straight.

dependencies.rationale: Direct refutation of the opening Ancient Arrow story.

claims.text: Even though the Wing Makers narrative was invented as fiction-marketed-as-fact and James Mahu has admitted it is mythology rather than history, the story can still have positive value because it has demonstrably pushed thousands of people toward legitimate pursuits like meditation, epigenetics, and consciousness studies.

claims.rationale: This is a values claim — fiction sold as fact can have downstream educational benefits — and reasonable people disagree. The Verne/Clarke/Star Trek analogy conflates labelled science fiction with materials marketed to credulous buyers as classified disclosures, which is a meaningful ethical distinction. The defense is fairer than 'pure scam' but the host is also overcharitable. Omitting directTruth because this is a values judgment, not a factual one.

claims.text: Despite the Wing Makers narrative being mythological invention, the underlying message — that humans are capable of more than they currently know — is true and is the legitimate reason the show's audience continues to seek out unconventional ideas.

evidence.quote:
stories always

me today. Hey, my name is AJ. That cycle
fish.
>> This has been the W files. If you get
fu

claims.rationale: The host concedes the literal claims are false and pivots to a vague aspirational moral ('we're capable of more than we know'), which is true in a banal sense — there's always more to learn. But it papers over a real harm: people paying $108 for a CD because they thought it contained alien-derived consciousness technology. The 'we're capable' framing is hard to dispute as written but is the kind of statement that flatters everyone and clarifies nothing.

dependencies.rationale: Builds on the host's own admission that the chambers and artifacts never existed.
